draft.introduction: Zeno of Elea (c. 490–430 BCE) devised his paradoxes to defend Parmenides’s monism by showing that plurality and motion lead to absurdity. The Achilles paradox argues that the fastest runner can never overtake the slowest.

translation.text: The slowest will never be overtaken in running by the fastest. For the pursuer must first reach the point from which the pursued started, so that the slower must always hold a lead.
translation.original_text: τὸ βραδύτατον οὐδέποτε καταληφθήσεται κατὰ τὴν δίωξιν ὑπὸ τοῦ ταχίστου· ἔμπροσθεν γὰρ ἀναγκαῖον ἐλθεῖν τὸν διώκοντα ὕθεν ὥρμησε τὸ φεῦγον

translator_notes.note: The paradox survives only through Aristotle’s account in the Physics. Aristotle believed it could be resolved by distinguishing potential from actual infinity, but the problem continued to challenge mathematicians until the development of convergent series.
